david wanted to know his exact military value he wanted to feel like an invincible conqueror

close attention to the way characters speak to each other in these ancient texts what does he

massive amount about the characters not just by what they say but by contrasting or contrasting or
comparing how long or short their speeches are the tone they use and who is dominating the
conversation that is such a cool way to read a text it is like watching the power dynamics in

wrapped in deep political respect he is desperately trying to wake david up from this

speech with david's response which is almost non-existent exactly the amazing thing is the
writer of the text does not even give david a spoken reply the text does not record a long

joe ebb that is it end of discussion david just uses his absolute blunt power to crush
the conversation wow that silence speaks volumes he doesn't explain himself because he doesn't

yes and robert alter's theory shows us that david's stubborn brief refusal to even listen perfectly

when a leader suddenly refuses to listen to good advice from their most trusted advisors

it is a glaring red flag that their ego has completely taken over
david has built a wall around himself and he is completely closed off to reason so despite his

it was gone it was no longer part of the nation oh wow so the writer of the book of

the fact that the book of samuel naturally uses from dan to beersheba proves that it has authentic

david felt very bad about what he did and realized he was wrong but that is emotionally

then let me read the choices for you so you can really feel the weight of this decision
choice number one seven years of severe famine in the land

a threshing floor actually is because most of us today have never been on an ancient farm that is a
very important detail to visualize in the ancient agricultural world a threshing floor was an
essential part of life
elevated area often made of solid rock or very hard packed dirt so it's a very practical workspace
exactly after the local farmers harvested their wheat from the fields they would bring the long
stalks up to this flat floor they would use heavy wooden instruments often called threshing sledges
which had sharp stones or iron teeth on the bottom that sounds intense it was they would
have oxen pull these heavy sledges over the wheat over and over again this violent crushing process
would break the good useful nutritious grain away from the useless dry outer shell which is called
the chaff and the reason they put the threshing floor on a high hill is because of the wind
right that's a key part once the wheat was totally crushed the farmers would take pitchforks
and toss the mixture high into the air the heavy good grain would fall straight down to the ground
but the strong wind blowing across the hill would catch the light useless chaff and blow it
completely away
exactly so if you think about it symbolically a threshing floor is fundamentally a place of
separation it is a place of intense purification it involves crushing weight and it involves the
wind blowing away the worthless parts it's where the valuable is separated from the worthless yes
